phase0_notes: Kinds below are the deployment-guide §1.3 taxonomy seeded as a baseline. A future Phase 0 taxonomy-discovery LLM should propose a game-specific refinement of this list, and a human must flip `human_approved` to true before Phase 1 ingest runs in production.

building.description: Stationary structures that players construct to defend colonies, produce resources, or unlock technologies.

unit.description: Soldiers and heroes that players command to defend against infected swarms.

character.description: Named campaign protagonists who lead defenses against infected invasions.

infected.description: Hostile creatures that attack player colonies in waves.

mechanic.description: Core gameplay systems that govern economy, combat, or progression.

wonder.description: Powerful late-game buildings with unique abilities that significantly impact gameplay.

organization.description: Factions and groups involved in the campaign narrative.

location.description: Named regions and areas within the game world.

campaign_map.description: Designed challenge maps with predetermined objectives and conditions.

survival_map.description: Procedurally-varied maps where players defend against endless infected waves.

engine_candidates.name: Godot 4
pros: Lightweight 2D engine specifically optimized for isometric tower defense
Built-in tilemap system and mature procedural generation libraries for survival map seeding
GDScript enables rapid iteration on complex game mechanics (building placement, unit pathfinding, wave logic)
Excellent entity management for dense populations (43 building types, 28 infected variants, swarm AI)
Open-source with zero runtime costs; strong precedent in indie strategy games
Fast reload cycle supports tight game-design feedback loops
cons: Smaller plugin ecosystem than Unity (fewer pre-built RTS/tower defense systems)
GDScript knowledge doesn't transfer outside Godot
Community is growing but smaller for advanced colony-management patterns
Networking requires third-party solutions (not a blocker for single-player focus)

engine_candidates.name: Bevy
pros: ECS architecture perfectly models entity-heavy swarms (natural representation of 28 infected types commanding coordinated groups)
Deterministic simulation guarantees reproducible procedural maps with fixed seeds (critical for survival mode verification)
Rust's type system prevents mutation bugs across complex building/unit/infected interactions
Exceptional real-time performance under high entity count; zero garbage collection pauses during wave simulation
Strong procedural generation and pathfinding crates (bevy_xpbd, pathfinding)
cons: Newer ecosystem with fewer finished tower defense reference implementations
Rust learning curve steeper than GDScript; slower team onboarding
UI framework less mature (Colony resource HUD requires custom work vs. market solutions)
Smaller community for debugging colony-specific mechanics (balancing infected waves, economy)

engine_candidates.name: Unity
pros: Industry standard with mature tower defense precedent (numerous shipped tower defense titles)
Rich asset marketplace for pathfinding, procedural generation, and colony UI templates
uGUI framework purpose-built for resource management HUDs (economy, population, research tech trees)
Netcode ecosystem ready if campaign co-op is added later
Large existing team expertise in C#; rapid prototyping through UI Builder
cons: Heavier runtime than needed for 2D isometric scope; increased binary size and memory footprint
Per-seat licensing; potential revenue-share obligations above threshold
Over-engineered for tower defense scope; managing 3D infrastructure overhead for 2D game
Slower iteration cycle due to compilation and scene management complexity

engine_candidates.name: Unreal Engine 5
pros: Mature engine with professional-grade AI systems (Unreal's Behaviour Trees suit complex infected swarm coordination)
Exceptional performance pipeline for rendering and physics under dense entity scenarios
Strong C++ ecosystem for deterministic simulation (fixed-point arithmetic, seeded RNG)
Scalable architecture if campaign narrative expands or multiplayer is added
Established talent pool; lower recruitment friction for experienced C++ engine programmers
cons: Significantly overspecified for 2D isometric tower defense scope
C++ compilation overhead slows iteration; Blueprints add project complexity
Large disk and memory footprint (2D game does not justify Nanite/Lumen infrastructure)
Steep learning curve; 3D-first tools create friction for 2D isometric design
Smaller isometric RTS community within Unreal ecosystem

chosen_engine.name: Bevy
chosen_engine.architecture: fixed-tick sim (20-30Hz) decoupled from interpolated render tick; only inputs cross the wire
chosen_engine.notes: Determinism rules are binding on all Phase 2 sim-path code. See CLAUDE.md > 'Target engine' for the full rule set.
